questions.question: Explain the concept of pipeline architecture in microcontrollers with its advantages.
keyPoints: Definition: technique to overlap execution of multiple instructions by dividing into stages
Each stage handled by dedicated hardware unit
Advantages: increased throughput, improved performance, better resource utilization
First instruction takes k cycles, subsequent complete every cycle

questions.question: List and briefly explain the five stages of classic RISC pipeline.
keyPoints: IF - Instruction Fetch: fetch instruction from memory
ID - Instruction Decode: decode instruction, read registers
EX - Execute: perform ALU operation, calculate address
MEM - Memory Access: access data memory for load/store
WB - Write Back: write result back to register file

questions.question: What are the three types of pipeline hazards? Explain each with an example.
keyPoints: Structural Hazard: hardware resource conflict (e.g., single memory port)
Data Hazard: instruction depends on result of previous (RAW, WAR, WAW)
Control Hazard: branch/jump changes flow (e.g., conditional branch outcome unknown)

questions.question: Define pipeline stall and pipeline flush. How do they differ?
keyPoints: Pipeline Stall: insertion of NOP/bubble cycles to delay instruction until hazard resolved
Pipeline Flush: clearing of incorrectly fetched instructions when hazard detected
Stall delays execution; flush discards already fetched instructions
Flush commonly occurs in branch misprediction; stall in data dependencies

questions.question: Calculate the speedup for a 5-stage pipeline executing 100 instructions. Assume ideal conditions with no hazards.
keyPoints: Non-pipeline time: 100 × 5 = 500 cycles
Pipeline time: 5 + 100 - 1 = 104 cycles (fill + drain + execution)
As n→∞, speedup approaches 5 (number of stages)

questions.question: Explain the technique of forwarding (data bypassing) to resolve data hazards.
keyPoints: Forwarding passes results directly from execute/memory stages to subsequent instructions
Eliminates need to wait until write-back stage completes
ALU result from EX/MEM stage forwarded to EX stage of dependent instruction
Load result forwarded from MEM/WB to EX stage (may still need stall for load-use hazard)

questions.question: What is the difference between pipeline latency and pipeline throughput?
keyPoints: Latency: time for single instruction to traverse all pipeline stages (in cycles)
Throughput: number of instructions completed per unit time (instructions per cycle)
Throughput = 1 IPC in ideal pipeline after filling
Latency = number of stages × clock period; Throughput = 1/latency (ideally)

questions.question: Draw and explain a 5-stage pipeline timing diagram for executing 4 instructions. Also derive the pipeline speedup formula.
keyPoints: Diagram showing 4 instructions I1,I2,I3,I4 across 5 stages with proper overlap
Show fill period (first 4 cycles), steady state (1 instruction/cycle), drain period
Non-pipeline cycles = n × k = 4 × 5 = 20
Pipeline cycles = k + n - 1 = 5 + 4 - 1 = 8
Speedup = (n×k)/(k+n-1), approaches k as n→∞
Explain with calculation showing approximately 4.8x speedup

questions.question: Explain all three types of pipeline hazards in detail. For each hazard, suggest suitable solutions to overcome them.
keyPoints: Structural: solution - duplicate hardware resources, multi-port memory, proper scheduling
Data: solution - forwarding/bypassing, register renaming, compiler scheduling, stall insertion
Control: solution - branch prediction, delayed branching, flush on misprediction, loop unrolling
Provide examples for each solution with timing diagrams where applicable

questions.question: Explain the concept of pipeline speedup and efficiency. Calculate: (a) Speedup for 8-stage pipeline with 50 instructions (b) What is maximum possible speedup?
keyPoints: Speedup = Non-pipeline time / Pipeline time = n×k / (k+n-1)
Efficiency = Actual speedup / Ideal speedup × 100%
Part (a): Non-pipeline = 50×8 = 400, Pipeline = 8+50-1 = 57, Speedup = 400/57 ≈ 7.02
Part (b): Maximum speedup = k = 8 (as n→∞)
Discuss reasons for less than ideal speedup: fill/drain overhead

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the organization of a typical microcontroller pipeline. Discuss the role of each stage and potential hazards in such pipelines.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Fetch → Decode → Execute → Memory → Write-back
IF: PC, MAR, Memory, IR; ID: Register file, Control unit; EX: ALU; MEM: Data memory; WB: Register file
Harvard vs Von Neumann impact on structural hazards
Microcontroller-specific hazards: memory-mapped I/O access, interrupt handling in pipeline
Solutions: delayed load, branch delay slots, hazard detection unit
